comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Apollonius of Tyana, one of the most famous occultists of the ancient Roman world, who was among other things one of the first people we know of who revived an extinct occult tradition. As a teenager he read about the Pythagorean Brotherhood, and decided that he wanted to be a member. The minor fact that the Brotherhood had been extinct for most of five centuries didn't slow him down at all; he found out everything that was known about them, and proceeded to put himself through the traditional course of training, including the five year vow of silence. Approach occultism with that kind of determination and I promise you that you'll get good results; he certainly did. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.    

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Mothers are notoriously inaccurate as a source of birth times. That's understandable, as they're usually very busy doing something other than watching the clock! Of the witnesses, your grandparents are least likely to be flustered at the time, so they tend to be a little more accurate. Still, you need to do some rectification to find out. (a) might be a synchronicity or it might be chance. As for (b), that's one way to go about it; another is to learn how to progress your chart -- the basic rule is that each day (as in, exactly 24 hours ) minutes 0 seconds) after birth corresponds to one year. Take the year of your first serious illness, progress your probable birth charts to that year (by taking the chart as many days after your birth as you were years old at the time), and see if that brings the Moon or one of the swift-moving planets into a hostile aspect with another planet in the chart. Do the same for the year you lost your virginity and see whether you get an aspect involving Venus, and so on.

2) Sure, but the mistakes are part of the learning curve!

Thanks very much for doing this, John!

Some background on my question: I’ve been reading your blogs for several years, mostly for your political and philosophical content, but I’ve found these magic Monday sessions to make for fascinating reading. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a valid question, of course, but the answer has to come from within you. To put things in Christian language, each soul is called to God along a different path; mystical paths such as Hesychasm, magical paths such as the Golden Dawn, and the path of ordinary service in everyday life are among the countless options. Since I'm an occultist and not a mystic, I'm not really qualified to compare what I do with Hesychasm, or any other mystical path.

What I'd encourage you to do is read books from various paths that appeal to you, and read biographies of people who've committed themselves to those paths. Ask yourself: are these practices the sort of thing I want to spend my life doing? Is this person the kind of person I want to become? Combine that with prayer -- lots of prayer -- and you'll find the right path for you.

replies.title: Re: Pathworking
replies.content: I'd encourage you to do ahead and pursue Pathworking as an initiatory practice first, letting the opening of each Path have its natural effect on your subtle bodies. Don't get into the practical applications right away, or you'll hinder the broader process of inner development. Also, be careful about that slippery word/concept "motivation." Motivation is a means, not an end; what are you trying to motivate yourself to do? Are you sure that it's a good idea, and that there aren't good reasons why you're not motivated to do it? Meditation, divination, and journaling on questions along those lines may just spare you some grief.

1. I recently started reading Dion Fortune's novel The Sea Priestess. What a great book! Early on, her protagonist Wilfred writes: ...and it seemed to me that there were two worships, one of the sun, and one of the moon; and that my love of the moon and the sea was the older and was to the other as the other is to us. Is the lunar/sea magic described in the Sea Priestess similar to the Druid magic system in the DMH, given the importance of the lunar current in Druid magic of that system? I'm curious why she refers to Druidry as a sun-cult (and thus a solar-current based system).

2. I recently came across this quote by a scholar named Gregory Cajete which alludes to your posts made about tamanous on the other blog, and also neatly connects the 5 elements of the DMH: It is in the landscape that contains the memories, the bones of the ancestors, the earth, air, fire, water, and spirit from which a Native culture has come and to which it continually returns. It is the land that ultimately defines a Native people. I can't find the source. Have you come across this quote before?

Thanks as always!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, the old Atlantean sea magic is something else again. If the traditional lore is right, it was the "old religion" in Atlantis, overlaid by a sun-cult that came in with agriculture and the building of pyramids. The system in the DMH is later still, a blending of the solar magic with an earth-based rather than sea-based magic from points much further east. As for the ancient Druids, well, it depends on who you ask; we don't know a lot about them, but my best guess is that they came into being out of a bunch of disparate influences: a pre-Celtic sun cult with Atlantean roots, the folk religion the Celts brought with them from their Indo-European ancestors, and then an overlay of Pythagorean and proto-Pythagorean teachings that spread north from Massilia (modern Marseilles), as Nora Chadwick suggests in her very good book on the Druids.

2) No, I've never encountered that before. It's a very traditional statement, though!

comments.title: Sphere of Protection questions
comments.content:
Does it matter in what direction the sphere spins, or if you change directions?

Is it ok to use Awen in the final part if you are not invoking Celtic gods?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection questions
replies.content:
1) It's best to choose a set of directions and stick to them, but which ones you choose doesn't seem to matter much.

2) Druids don't just invoke Celtic gods! Druidry, especially modern Druidry, is open to just about any form of worship you care to name; I've met Christian Druids, Heathen Druids who worship Norse or Anglo-Saxon deities, Shinto Druids, Hindu Druids, you name it. Druidry is more a philosophy and a spiritual path than a religion as such, so it makes room for a great many religious variations. All of which is to say that sure, it's okay to use the word Awen; as the word by which the worlds were made, it embraces all.

comments.content: You often refer to the 'subtle bodies'. May I ask what that means - what are the subtle bodies? Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In occult philosophy, your physical body -- the one made of meat, bone, blood, etc. -- is only one of several bodies you have. The others are less perceptible to the ordinary senses, so they're called the subtle bodies. Along with your physical body, you have an etheric body, which is the body of life force -- what martial artists call qi or ki and practitioners of yoga call prana. You also have an astral body, which is the body you experience in your dreams, and also plays a role in imagination, most kinds of thinking, and memory. Finally, you have a mental sheath -- not yet a mental body, though it will become that eventually -- which is the body through which you relate to the universe of meaning. There are other levels beyond the mental, but you don't have bodies on those planes yet; so the etheric body, the astral body, and the mental sheath make up your subtle bodies.

replies.title: Aura
replies.content: What is the aura?  Is it the astral body?

replies.title: Re: Aura
replies.content: It's the part of the subtle bodies that extends outside the boundaries of the physical body. What clairvoyants call the "health aura," which extends an inch or two outside your skin, is part of the etheric body; the other layers of the aura, reaching out to the egg-shaped zone the Golden Dawn called the sphere of sensation, are part of the astral body. When we finally develop mental bodies those will extend further still.

replies.title: Re: Herbal Medicine
replies.content:
If I may:

I highly recommend starting with both volumes of Matthew Wood's Earthwise Herbal. Studying those alone for a few months is about as good as shelling out a few hundred dollars for an intensive herbal class. If you want to move herbalism in a magical direction, Culpepers _Complete Herbal_ is indispensable as is our hosts _Encyclopedia of Natural Magic_ and Cat Yronwode's _Hoodoo Herbal_ book. Maud Grieve's A Modern Herbal is vital for folklore. For serious practice, Finley Ellingwood's _American Materia-Medica and Pharmacognosy_ is the 1915 medical textbook of choice. If you click on my username it will take you to some of my own internet published herbal musings, which, are of now, more esoterically focused.

Question about a house chart being perfected by company:

Say you've got Puer in the 1st house, and Puella in the 6th, regarding a question about acute illness. They show up nowhere else in the chart, but Laetitia is in 2 and Cauda Draconis is in 5, paired up via Fire line with the significators. They also are nowhere else in the chart.

Is this considered "company capitular" by itself, or do the neighbors have to relate in some way? Laetitia in 2 does make a trine with Puella in 6, and then Cauda in 5 also makes a trine with Puer in 1; is that perfection by favorable aspect what's needed to complete the company capitular?

General thoughts on what this might be saying? Obviously kidney and/or lower back problems. Aquisitio is in 4 if that helps. Carcer is in 10 but I'm not seeing anything that moves the answer that direction.

Just a little confused...

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: This isn't perfection by company, because company only applies between figures in adjacent houses. Laetitia in the 2nd and Cauda Drac in the 5th can't have company between them. Whatever you were asking about the illness, the answer is "no." With Puer in the first, you probably jumped to an inaccurate conclusion on the basis of too little evidence, and with Puella in the 6th, as a stable figure and a beneficial one, there's nothing seriously wrong.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Thank you for the clarification! And that's good news indeed.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
You're welcome, and I'm glad to hear it.

In dealing with company, the crucial thing to keep in mind -- burn this brutally into your backside with a branding iron! -- is that company doesn't matter unless the two figures are next to each other, and in paired houses. Outside of that situation, it means nothing, nada, zip, zilch, zero. Two figures with the same first line in the 2nd and 3rd houses aren't in company, much less two figures in houses that aren't adjacent at all!

I did a house chart of the "Will X occur if I do Y?" variety that perfected by translation.

I then did Y and X did not occur. After checking the math, I assumed that I had simply gotten the answer wrong.

But then, while reviewing the section on translation in your Art and Practice of Geomancy, it occurred to me that I may have misinterpreted the meaning of a translation, so I wanted to ask for clarification.

The text talks about an outside factor bringing about a result the querent could not manage on their own. I assumed this meant, "The answer is yes, and here is more information about how things will work out," and thus that the third factor was certain to come into the situation regardless of any further action on the part of the querent.

But I realized translation might be a conditional answer: "The answer is yes, if the third factor comes into play," with the details of the translating figure indicating how that could happen and whether or not further action was required.

In this case, I did not identify the meaning of the translating figure (Conjunctio 2nd and 4th, not passing to any others), but to be honest, I kind of skimmed over that part because of how I was interpreting translation.

So, was I correct in my initial reading (translation is a yes with extra details) and I simply got it wrong, or am I correct in my revision (translation is a conditional yes, and interpreting the translating figure is essential)?

Thank you very much!

replies.title: Re: translating woes

comments.title: Pluto status
comments.content:
Apologies if this is a stupid newbie question. There's been considerable talk in these pages about the influence of Pluto waning. But why would that be? It's still the same rock orbiting the sun that it's always been. Why does it make a difference whether astronomers classify it as a planet? It would seem to have a significant influence in my natal chart (it's conjunct my sun, and I do have a tendency to be secretive and silent) and some other charts I've looked at, but now I'm uncertain how to interpret it.

Also, a minor question--would you consider a grand trine to be present if one of the points is the Ascendant rather than a planet?

replies.title: Re: Pluto status
replies.content: John Michael's notion that Pluto's influence is waning is completely theoretical at this stage...only time will tell. It may be an entirely alien concept for the majority of astrologers. Sun conjunct Pluto is a powerful aspect (I have Sun opposite Pluto) and you will most likely always identify with many Plutonian themes and feel its intensity any time one of the outer planets make hard/challenging aspects by transit. Regarding your second question, that certainly qualifies as a grand trine in the texts I've studied.

replies.title: Re: Pluto status
replies.content: If I may -- no, it's not completely theoretical. It's based on a fair amount of experience, including watching what was supposed to be a really disruptive Pluto transit aspecting two planets in my natal chart pass by without any of the dire effects those aspects supposedly predict, and also watching a whole series of predictions of sudden economic calamity backed by Pluto conjunctions go past with an equal lack of effect. If Pluto was all it's cracked up to be, those shouldn't have happened...and yet if you look at the mundane charts for the Aries ingress in 1929, the one that governed the beginning of the Second World War, Pluto is hugely important (it was rising in London and Paris, with crucial aspects to other important planets). I also know people who went through Pluto transits back before 2006 and had a horrible time with them. Something has clearly changed; my thesis is an attempt to account for the changes.

replies.title: Re: Pluto status
replies.content:
1) It's been noticed repeatedly that the discovery of a new planet marks the emergence of new patterns in human society -- for example, the discovery of Uranus correlates precisely with the rise of the ideals of democracy and individual liberty. Uranus was there all along, but its influence became markedly more powerful when it was discovered. Similarly, other bodies that were thought to be planets -- the minor planet Ceres is a great example -- had new patterns in society emerge when they were discovered, and fade out when they were downgraded to less-than-planet status. I've noticed that the same thing seems to be happening with Pluto. Sure, the rock is the same, but human collective attitudes (including the attitudes of astronomers) are affected by astrological factors; the decision of the astronomers didn't affect the planet, the planet affected the decision of the astronomers -- just as it did in the 1850s, when Ceres, the planet of the Romantic movement, was downgraded and took the movement with it.

2) Nope. It's worth noting, but according to the texts I've studied, if there isn't a planet there, it's not a grand trine.

replies.title: Re: Pluto status
replies.content: Do you know anything which discusses the link between Ceres and the Romantic Era in detail?

replies.title: Re: Pluto status
replies.content: Nope. I'll be writing about it in due time.

replies.title: Re: Pluto status
replies.content: Another minor planet worth careful study. She clearly got the right name, since she threw the whole field of planetary astrology into confusion the way Eris threw the gods and goddesses into confusion!

comments.title: Magic and family problems
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This might be a stretch for a magic question but I am wondering about your perspective - apologies for rambling. I've been practicing LRM, just started the Middle Pillar and overall felt I was beginning to make progress. I've been in a discouraging work situation too but had gotten together a plan to hustle over the next few months and get on track. However, I recently got news that my estranged father has a tumor and feel lost.

The short story of my family situation is my mother is depressive and religious and everything in the family revolved around fulfilling her neediness, and this is still true. But technically it was not abusive, so I feel guilty, just I became gradually more estranged as every relationship failed to develop and I became more labelled as the family black sheep anyway. I've had constant feelings of being different from others because I don't "have a family", but when I tried to keep in contact, it was the same, because of the lack of closeness and sense of acting on an unfair duty. So now it is a crisis, stuck in anxiety. With the momentary "resolution" that I explained my feelings to my sister and she wrote back with a lot of hostility.

The questions I was thinking:

1) Is it possible that the Middle Pillar could have precipitated my father's illness? I had very intense, weird dreams with something about water rising and dangerous necromantic magic etc the night before I got the news. I think most likely it is a coincidence, and he is in his late 70's so an illness is not unusual, but I decided until his surgery to hold off on MP and instead pray twice a day for insight and for the best resolution for each to come about.

2) My mother's Mars in Aries is almost right on my South Node. Does that mean that all the problems with this over the years is likely because I "owe her" karmically from a past life?

3) I feel like practicing any form of spirituality is ridiculous because I am a terrible person, beyond anyone I know, who all have some connection to their family. But I guess that is not right and it's still worthwhile to banish etc and seek improvement?

4) When I journal, I seem to come to an impasse of damned if I do or don't. I guess - journal about that?

replies.title: Re: Magic and family problems
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, sit down, have a beverage of your choice, put your feet up, and relax a bit. When you say "it wasn't technically abuse" I don't believe you; you're behaving like someone who suffered a lot of emotional abuse. It's a common pattern in abusive families to assign one child the role of "black sheep," and do everything possible to convince that child that he or she is a terrible person, etc., and is automatically to blame for anything wrong that happens to anyone else in the family. It's a crock of crap, but it can take a long time for the child abused in that way to outgrow it.

Now on to the questions.

1) No. Practicing the Middle Pillar exercise never gave anyone else any illness, ever. The fact that you blame yourself for his illness shows me how much emotional abuse you must have suffered.

2) No. The South Node refers to your past, especially past experiences you haven't really processed and dealt with; it includes past life issues but isn't limited to that. Her Mars conjunct your South Node means that you have a really hard time dealing with your past experiences involving her, and that she has a very easy time manipulating and hurting you by way of those unresolved experiences.

3) All the people I've ever met who were convinced they were terrible people were actually really nice people who'd been subjected to emotional abuse by somebody. All the people I've ever met, without exception, who were genuinely terrible, vile, nasty people were convinced that they were wonderful and could do no wrong. I really doubt that you're an exception to that rule. Of course you should practice spirituality; it's intended for us imperfect human beings, not for paragons of perfection.

4) You might want to do some positive affirmations to counteract some of the awful programming you've been subjected to by your family -- but yes, using journaling to confront the garbage you've been taught about yourself is a useful tool.

May I add something else? I haven't spoken to my mother in nearly thirty years, and with any luck, I will never have to speak to her again. Some people shouldn't be allowed to raise goldfish; unfortunately some of them end up raising children. You have my profound sympathy; I had a different kind of ghastly childhood and dysfunctional family than the one you did, but it's obvious that yours also really sucked, and it's going to take much time and healing to deal with the wounds and scars left by that sort of experience.

replies.title: Re: Magic and family problems
replies.content: My sympathies to both of you.

replies.title: Re: Magic and family problems
replies.content:
Thanks much JMG. I’ve definitely been in a panic loop and you’ve put the sun back in my day and stemmed the “give up” impulse.

Very glad MP is safe and I can pick it back up! I seem blocked in the heart area and it felt to be helping even after a few times.

Your south node interpretation is certainly less strained.

This sense of being terrible and “damned” does seem excessive, and not really born out by life. I’d been holding off on affirmations because of not being really sure in what area to address. This definitely announced itself as most pressing.

Thank you for sharing about your situation... I can see that since I respect you just as much with family struggles, it doesn’t really make sense to be so self judgmental.

Again many many thanks! I will stop despairing and keep on magicking.

comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Last Friday, my sister and I were contacted by a kind of entity that's very new to us. She'd just finished giving me a tarot reading and we decided to use a pendulum to see if anybody without a body wanted to chat. Through a lot of "yes" and "no" responses, we learned there was a guardian spirit protecting me that was not a ghost or an angel. We learned the spirit was not a god, but it was a deity. We went through the alphabet to try to figure out the name and got an "H". My sister is a medium, so she asked to communicate to to it directly and learned the name of the Greek Goddess "Hecate," which she had never heard before and I was only vaguely familiar with. My sister is in her 40s and has only ever communicated with ghosts, angels, and the occasional annoying demon, so even the existence of a pagan god was something of a shock to her--and I had been wondering how much of my interaction with deities was just my own psychology until a new one popped up, fully formed and made itself known. Since then, my sister and I have communicated with Hecate and gotten useful direction on a number of life issues in just a couple days. Despite the associations she has with black magic and necromancy, she's appeared to us as a very wise, loving figure and is very supportive of my magical practice and desire for self-improvement and transformation.

This is the first deity that's every really talked to me, so I have questions:

1) Hecate said I should invoke her as part of my sphere of protection, which confused me, because I already have a pantheon for the SoP, and none of those gods are Greek, and it doesn't feel right to kick any of them out. Something I've been doing for all my ritual work has been the "book-opening" and "book-closing" hand gestures I got from Learning Ritual Magic to "book-end" all of my practices. I decided to add a verbal component to this, announcing the opening hand gesture as the "Grimoire of Hecate" with a suitable benediction, and then performing the SoP in it's entirety with no alterations, and then performing the closing hand gesture with the same words.

Is this going to change the effect of the SoP in any detrimental way? So far, the effect has been stabilizing and empowering and made me more sensitive to Hecate's influence, but I'm pretty new to all of this, so I feel better asking you.

2) Is the connection my sister and I have to Hecate the kind of Outer Plane Contact you talk about as a requisite for lodge or other group work? We're not planning on starting a coven anytime soon, and we live several states away (I was visiting), but I am curious.

3) The Goddess specifically chose the name Hecate when she contacted us, not Ceridwen, Ishtar, Kali, Shakti, or any of the other non-dualistic dark mothers, which gives me the impression that she is distinct from them. Do you know anything about the relationship between similar gods in different pantheons? Are they different aspects of a single entity, or are they closely aligned like a sisterhood?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Talk to her and ask her how she wants you to include her in the SoP. Her advice will be more to the point than mine!

2) I think you've gotten several things confused here -- an outer plane check, which is something in the world of everyday experience that confirms something received from the inner, and an inner plane contact, which is something that some people who do magical lodge work use. If you'll examine my book Inside a Magical Lodge, you'll find that I say in so many words that you do not need an inner plane contact to have a functioning magical lodge!

3) That's one of the big questions in polytheist religion, and opinions vary. They certainly appear to act as though they're different beings. Beyond that, nobody knows.

Sometimes these things can linger for a very long time, long after the peopkle who called it up have gone elsewhere or died out. I don't think anyone knows for certain why such things do linger in some places (or houses), but do not leave any perceptible trace in other places (or houses). It just seems to be so. My own best explanation is that it's a thing the place (or house) decides for itself, something rather like a choice.

And I very strongly recommend using enormous caution and extreme skepticism about any recovered memory of traume that might surface due to your unsatisfied curiosity about what lay behind the dreams you had. More likely than not, it never was about you or about anything you underwent in that house.

comments.title: Pachamama and the Amazon Synod
comments.content:
In Catholic news, there has been a synod in Rome, in which the people of the Amazon and their beliefs have been discussed. It has ignited a bit of a firestorm on social media, with conservatives worried about pagan influences and liberals accusing conservatives of racism.

replies.title: Re: Pachamama and the Amazon Synod
replies.content:
Pachamama is a pagan deity -- the Andean earth-mother goddess. To have the Catholic hierarchy setting up statues of her in Catholic churches is precisely equivalent to setting up statues of Demeter or Ceres there -- or, say, setting up a statue of Jupiter in the Temple of Jerusalem, as in the Book of Maccabees. It's been fairly clear to me that the Modernist faction in the Catholic church doesn't actually believe in the god its members claim to worship or the teachings they claim to uphold, and I think this pretty much proves it.

If people want to worship pagan deities, I'm all in favor of it -- I do that myself, you know -- but it would be less confusing if the people who do that would stop pretending to be Roman Catholics. As for "racist," these days that word means "something a liberal doesn't like," full stop, end of sentence. The people who threw the images into the river weren't being racist, they were protesting the desecration of a church by the installation of a pagan idol there. (For that matter, I wonder what Pachamana thought about having her statues put into a church? With the long and ugly history of persecution of pagans in Latin America, I doubt she was well pleased...)

replies.title: Re: Affirmations

comments.title: "When torn, don't choose" and some random questions
comments.content:
Greetings JMG!

Since I started anything spiritual two years ago, the great benefits aside, an anxiety also started to grow inside me, which I think other spiritual seekers of the click-and-sink era of information also feel, so I thought I'd write about what I found out by meditating and reading some other responses here about it.
For the past months, since I was filled with intrigue by the introduction you wrote for Levi's book, which eventually took me here, I have been increasingly anxious to actually start and commit to something to get The Great Work kick started and ride away from ignorance into magical realms. As the initial rush of greater possibilities and the sudden bewilderment by the existence of spirits, angels and gods started to fade away I found myself with a mess of jigsaw pieces on the floor --all from different sets and with the possibility of lethal combination should I proceed to jam them together. Just choose goddammit! That was my initial emotional reaction. Why so of a sudden I have so many possibilities all equally fantastic? Why when I start to feel sure I'll do this or that out of nowhere something else appears that seems much better? So after cleaning the mess, putting the pieces back in their boxes and the insistent appearance of the fool on my castings I realized that I was getting exactly what I wanted and needed. I don't need to choose just now, but rather as I start to recognize and get familiar with the hidden subtleties of life what I need to do is explore. In order to do that I just need to examine the very actions that put me in this confusing anxiety-feeding situation first and then listen carefully to that which is presenting itself to me --possibilities. Whether I decide to turn that into suffering speaks volumes of what I have to fix first before heading out the door and that is prepare to make a choice and own it. Seems so obvious now!

With that said, I do have some questions:
1) Praying with a lit candle has shown several-folds improvements. As soon after I finished to pray to a Hindu god to help me with my Shambhavi practice the swaying and rocking of my body increased in intensity and variety and I also got a message saying "connect with the elements, connect with the sun, connect with the plants". How can I do that, I'm still figuring out but I assume it has to do with working with elemental and astrological intelligences. I understand this rocking or jolting movements to be symptom of newcomers, similar to how a pipe that is not clean starts shaking when water goes through it. Do you know about this bodily movements? If so, could you elaborate a little? Could it be along the lines of changes in the inner planes by my practice manifesting in the physical plane? They also seem to have an epicenter in the different energy nodes along the spine so I assume it has to do with waking them up. I wonder if I can work with them or just let them be.
2) I was reading along a MM question regarding demons and my speakers, which where off, started to make invitational sounds. What I did was just to say that if you are a demon I don't want anything to do with you and then prayed for protection. A second after a notification from my email popped up on my screen that said "message received". Whether or not it was what I assume it was it triggered the question. How do one knows if what one is praying to is what they think it is and not something else replying? For example gods or spirits. If it was a demon why would it go when I said so instead of fooling me?

Thanks for another MM and have a great week!

replies.title: Re: "When torn, don't choose" and some random questions
replies.content:
Good. Yes, you got the message.

1) The rocking movements are apparently common in magical traditions from the Indian subcontinent, where they work with a much denser level of etheric energy than we do in the West. (That's not good or bad, just different.) I've seen the same explanation you have -- it's the nadis (the channels of prana) cleaning themselves out. Don't try to do anything with them, until and unless you can receive instruction from a qualified teacher -- you can mess yourself over really seriously by tinkering with the dense ethers if you don't know what you're doing.

2) Demons can't stand prayer, because prayer brings you into contact with divine beings. It wasn't that you told it to go, it's that you prayed for protection. The moral to this story is "pray often!"

comments.title: Roman Deities
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

On a previous Magic Monday, I noted being contacted by Ceres. I have continued to develop that relationship with daily prayers immediately following my practice of the opening and closing of the SOP.

I am becoming more interested in the Roman deities and would like to try integrating them into my practice of the SOP, especially as I start to introduce the elements. I would use Ceres as the deity with whom I have a personal connection in the opening, but I am having a surprisingly hard time figuring out the other gods to call upon.

Here's what I have so far:

Sky God: Jupiter
Earth Goddess: Tellus
Personal Connection: Ceres
Air: ??? (Cardea or Venti, perhaps?)
Fire: Vulcan (or Vesta?)
Water: Juturna? (Feels more correct for me than Neptune)
Earth: Ops? Or Ceres again?
Spirit Below: Tellus again?
Spirit Above: ???
Spirit Within: ??? (Genius?)

If you have any guidance, I would really appreciate it! I feel a little silly asking, as it doesn't seem like it should be that hard to figure this out, but I've spent a chunk of time looking through lists of Roman deities and have largely confused myself at this point. I am otherwise pretty ignorant of the Roman pagan religion, which is perhaps my problem!

Thanks so much, JMG!

replies.title: Re: Roman Deities
replies.content:
Okay, it's important to remember that the Sphere of Protection is flexible, and so there's a lot of room for flexibility in assigning deities to it. You don't have to follow elemental correspondences exactly! I'd very strongly encourage you to learn more about Roman myth (and also Greek myth, much of which was borrowed by the Romans). Pick up a copy of Bulfinch's Mythology to start with and read it, so you have a basic idea of who's who, and go from there. In the meantime:

Since Ceres is the goddess you're working with primarily, ignore the business about sky gods and earth goddesses and go straight to classical Roman religion. Ceres was one of three deities, the Aventine Triad, who were worshiped as a set in rites for agricultural fertility. The others were Liber and Libera. So your opening invocation, in good classical Latin, would run:

Liber, Libera, et Ceres! Dii immortales me audite!
(LEE-ber, LEE-ber-ah, et KEH-res! DE-ee imm-or-TAL-es meh OW-dee-tay!)
("Liber, Libera, and Ceres! Immortal deities, hear me!")

Your six deities for the SoP would then be as follows, drawing from the oldest Roman deities:

East: Mavors (originally, guardian of the herds, later pronounced Mars)
West: Quirinus (originally, guardian of the fields)
South: Vesta (goddess of the hearth fire)
North: Juturna (goddess of springs and water rising from the earth)
Spirit Below: Vejovis (the primordial earth deity, Jupiter's subterranean twin)
Spirit Above: Jupiter (the god of weather and thunder)

(Remember that J in Latin is always pronounced a Y and V as W, so Mawors, Westa, Yuturna, Weyowis, and Yuppiter.)

For Spirit Within, why, the Romans had that exact concept; every man had a guardian spirit called a Genius and every woman a comparable guardian spirit called a Juno, to whom they prayed regularly and made offerings on their birthdays.

You're going to be dealing with very ancient, primal, earthy powers here, but that's hardly an objection...

replies.title: Re: Roman Deities
replies.content:
Thank you! This is great information--better than I hoped--and makes a lot of sense. It also feels very good (we'll see how it feels when I actually do it). Your last line brings a smile to my face, too; I will aim to be careful, but those are the kind of powers I feel drawn to, so that's promising to me.

I'll have to work on my Latin pronunciation. I have been pronouncing Ceres in the English way. Very interesting to realize that the Latin pronunciation is nearly the same as my last name. Also pleased to see Vesta and Juturna in there, as both of them grabbed my attention as I was perusing the list of Roman deities.

As for reading, I have a copy of Spaeth's book on Ceres on its way and just reserved Bulfinch's Mythology from the library. I'll continue perusing potential titles to bump up my knowledge.

Thanks again for all your help with this. It means a lot to me!

comments.content:
1. What are the advantages of a shield chart vs. a house chart (and vice versa) in geomancy? I've heard that it's unwise to use them in conjunction, so when should one be used over the other?

2. For the Sphere of Protection or Middle Pillar exercise etc., what would you recommend as far as invoking gods one doesn't have a relationship with? For instance, if I honor Odin but have never approached Freya, would it be bad manners to invoke her?

3. As a fellow Aspergian, are there any specific practices you've found to be the most helpful in managing the less helpful symptoms? I've worked steadily over the years to improve my social skills and confidence on a mundane level, but a magical boost would certainly be welcome.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The shield chart's simpler, and thus easier to teach and learn. The house chart gives you much more detail, but is accordingly harder to work with.

2) Get a bottle of good ale, open it, go outside, call to her, tell her that you'd be honored to invoke her in your ritual work, and pour out the aleto her. The Heathen gods and goddesses in particular tend to be very congenial when approached in that highly traditional way.

3) Not really. The one thing that really helped me wasn't magical; it was joining and being active in a couple of old-fashioned fraternal orders -- the Masons, the Odd Fellows, and the Grange. The very structured, formal way that people in those orders relate to each other was very helpful in giving me a framework to explore what worked and what didn't, and some common Aspie skills -- crazed intensity of focus and a really good memory for ritual texts -- are highly valued in that context.

comments.title: questions
comments.content:
Archdruid,

So a few different questions.

1) as I've said over the last several weeks, Ive been working with the Will over the past several weeks. On Sunday morning, during my invocation of fire (through which I am manifesting the will) I got a nose bleed.

Basically I had built up my will and intention to go to a martial arts class, but I redirected the energy toward other purposes. During the redirection I felt an intense pressure in my body, anxiety, a mild ache in the back of my head. Before all the other symptoms, my nose started bleeding. Each symptom faded in the reverse order that I listed them. Is this something I should be concerned about?

2) Last week I asked why older definitions of words were so much clearer, and you said that it was a symptom of the natural life cycle of civilization. That reminded me of two lessons from India and China, that relate to my questions.

The story from India talks about a youngman who spends many years training under a holyman. The youngman finally achieves the highest level of training and his teacher imparts the most secret mantra of the order, which grants the chanter unity with the Devas. The youngman is ordered by his teacher never to share the story with the uninitiated.

The youngman thinks is over and shares it anyway, and when confronted by his out raged teacher explains that it is better to share the path of the gods than keep it secret.

The teacher, with joyous tears in his eyes, embraces the student for his wisdom.

In the Chinese lessons, Lou tzu was once heard saying that the true meaning of a thing is lost the moment it is communicated.

I feel like these are occult lessons. The story from India never sat well with me, it's one of those things that's circulated by email and Whatsapp from friends and relatives, but it seems like spreading a mantra without the prior training would be a fast way to dissipate its power.

My questions are:
a) Am I on the right track to understanding the secret words of power?
b) Is this why mages usually created secret languages? To bind the world with words and meaning from their own perspective?
c) Is there a blueprint for such language in the Druid Magic Handbook?

3) One of my major hindrances to training is a series of bad habits I picked up through college. Because of the way the system was designed it was easy for me to find cracks, loopholes, and work-arounds that allowed me to avoid doing any real work. I passed with high grades and a good GPA, but I’ve found that my study skills for all subjects are weak and scattered. I want to become a great student, to the point where I can set myself to any subject and learn it without half-measures. How do I achieve this?

Regards, Syfen

replies.title: Re: questions
replies.content:
1) No, but you might want to go to the martial arts class next time; your subconscious threw quite the tantrum!

2) The Indian story strikes me as feelgood nonsense. Words of power are indeed weakened when bandied about freely. Yes, there are hints in the direction of a secret language of power in The Druid Magic Handbook, and in several other books of mine -- one of them more clearly than most, but no, I'm not going to say which one. Now -- and I mean this with utter seriousness -- do not speak of this again to anyone. Find what you have the capacity and destiny to find, and use it.

3) Choose one small part of a subject and study it with full intensity. Plunge into it. Do it the way you know it should be done. Take what you learn from that experience, and proceed from there. The path is made by walking.

comments.title: Geomancy course?
comments.content:
You've mentioned previous plans for a school of geomancy that didn't work out.

Would you consider opening a subscription based school on a similar model to the astrology posts to revive this idea?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy course?
replies.content: I'll put some thought into that possibility. I'd need to figure out a way that someone couldn't simply pay the subscription fee, download everything, and then post it on a pirate site somewhere else.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy course?
replies.content:
A (relatively) high fee, I.e., more than your average book will usually prevent people from sharing files. After all, why would you give away something that cost you a good deal of money.

Making the course a community rather than just a book will help reduce this. E.g., create a private forum for members of the course where they can discuss the course with you and with one another. The sense of community will reduce the desire to pirate, and you'll have people in the lookout for you should someone pirate the course.

A certification at the end of the course may reduce this too.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy course?
replies.content:
Pirates can always copy the text, but they can't replicate mentoring and feedback from a teacher or the community. I think that's the key thing from a course.

It seems like there is a lot of interest in geomancy judging from the questions. Hope this comes to fruit!

The internet is a much less efficient mode of distributing information than its fans like to think!

replies.title: Re: Geomancy course?

The community thing has been working out for Jason Miller's online course -- I believe Chris Warnock's courses are also doing well.

Another thing I thought of was perhaps using the AaPoG as the "course text", and basing the instruction more on guided feedback. Students who went through the whole basic level under your guidance then move on to the advanced level with new material would be unlikely to pirate anything.
